desc:
You got a glimpse of the sun in an open glade, but after a time the trees begin
to close in. They just appeared to be less dense and tangled from a distance.
Then you discover deep folds in the ground unexpectedly, like the ruts of giant
wheels or wide moats and sunken roads long disused and choked with brambles.
name: The Old Forest

desc:
You got a glimpse of the sun in an open glade, but after a time the trees begin
to close in. They just appeared to be less dense and tangled from a distance.
Then you discover deep folds in the ground unexpectedly, like the ruts of giant
wheels or wide moats and sunken roads long disused and choked with brambles.
name: The Old Forest

desc:
You got a glimpse of the sun in an open glade, but after a time the trees begin
to close in. They just appeared to be less dense and tangled from a distance.
Then you discover deep folds in the ground unexpectedly, like the ruts of giant
wheels or wide moats and sunken roads long disused and choked with brambles.
name: The Old Forest

desc:
You got a glimpse of the sun in an open glade, but after a time the trees begin
to close in. They just appeared to be less dense and tangled from a distance.
Then you discover deep folds in the ground unexpectedly, like the ruts of giant
wheels or wide moats and sunken roads long disused and choked with brambles.
name: The Old Forest

desc:
You got a glimpse of the sun in an open glade, but after a time the trees begin
to close in. They just appeared to be less dense and tangled from a distance.
Then you discover deep folds in the ground unexpectedly, like the ruts of giant
wheels or wide moats and sunken roads long disused and choked with brambles.
name: The Old Forest

desc:
You got a glimpse of the sun in an open glade, but after a time the trees begin
to close in. They just appeared to be less dense and tangled from a distance.
Then you discover deep folds in the ground unexpectedly, like the ruts of giant
wheels or wide moats and sunken roads long disused and choked with brambles.
name: The Old Forest

desc:
You got a glimpse of the sun in an open glade, but after a time the trees begin
to close in. They just appeared to be less dense and tangled from a distance.
Then you discover deep folds in the ground unexpectedly, like the ruts of giant
wheels or wide moats and sunken roads long disused and choked with brambles.
name: The Old Forest

desc:
You are walking in tall grass where you can see the tracks of many animals,
especially of the ones you enjoyed with a pint of good beer when you last ate in
your village inn. But, do you dare disturb the peace of this place?
name: The Hunting Place

desc:
You are walking in tall grass where you can see the tracks of many animals.
Eastwards you see the hills rolling softly into a mist.
name: The Hunting Place

desc:
This is a top of a hill, which seems to be somewhat darker than those elsewhere
in the Barrow-downs. The top is wide and flattened, like a shallow saucer with a
green mounded rim. In the midst stands a single tall stone. The stone is
shapeless yet significant: like a landmark, or a guarding finger. To the south a
woodland of light green trees can be spotted.
name: On the Top of the Hill

desc:
A secluded dell runs in a north-south fashion on the side of the hill. The
slopes are clad in small leaftrees giving shelter from the outside. A small
spring babbles in the green lush grass at the bottom of the canyon. The air is
filled with the scent of flowers drifting along the dell. A small cave entrance
can be seen in the western side of the canyon.
name: The Spring

desc:
The passage ends in a circular room constructed with small square pieces of
black stone. Three huge altars, facing north, east and west, are the only
furniture in the room. The altars are filled with runes which seems old and
powerful. Each altar has four steel rings, one in each corner, an arrangement so
that victims can be tied down to the altars.
name: The Sacrifice Room

desc:
The passage leads north and south. The walls and ceiling consists of large
square pieces of black stone. Upon them images of winged warriors and dragons
has been painted. There is no movement in the chilly dry air. Lampholders are
fitted in the walls approximately four yards apart.
name: The Sacrifice Passage

desc:
A twenty by twenty yards large hall has been duged out in the hill. The walls
and ceilings are made of large square pieces of black stone. A well with the
radius of about one yard leads downwards in the middle of the room. A whining
sound can be heard from the well, but it might be a chilly draft. To the north a
passage leads into the darkness.
name: The Sacrifice Hall

desc:
The stonewell continues up and down from this ledge. There is no way to get back
up, and down there is only darkness. On the southern side of the well there is
one black stone missing, leaving a hole large enough to crawl through,
southwards.
name: The Ledge

desc:
You fall about 100 yards towards a certain death, but before you smash into the
floor you notice that the bottom of the well is a large hall.
name: The Bottom of the Well

desc:
A very narrow tunnel has been carved out of the rock in a rough manner very
unlike the craftmanship of the dwarves. The air is damp, moist and a slight
draft can be felt from the north where there is only darkness. A flickering
light can be seen to the south where the tunnel extends into a cave.
name: The Tunnel

desc:
A small cave opens up on both sides of the tunnel. It is being lit by torches
fasten on the walls evenly spaced out to supply a soft gentle glow. The cave is
ten yards wide and a tall dwarf would scratch his head against the ceiling if he
was standing on his toes. There are a few small benches by the walls and small
tables in front of them. Old residues of rotten food lies on the tables.
name: The Meetingroom

desc:
Tunnels lead north, south, east and west. They have been carved out in a rough
fashion. The damp air is filled with a scent of garbage, seemingly coming from
the eastern tunnel. The southern exit is smoother and adorned with fresh leaves
and flowers.
name: The Tunnelcrossing

desc:
This is a hall, about twenty yards wide and thirty yards deep. The ceiling is
higher here than in the surrounding tunnels and rooms, some six feet up. The
floor is filled with fresh leaves, and a large wooden bed dominates the southern
part of the room. A mirror is placed besides the bed. The only visible exit is
north.
name: The Hall of the Hobwill Chief

desc:
The room is filled with garbage, and the smell leaves evidence of decay in the
air. The garbage must have been left here for years. Low squeeks can be heard
from inside the garbagepiles, perhaps the sound of rats. West of here a
tunnelcrossing can be seen.
name: The Garbage Room

desc:
This is a fairly large room, with ten small beds scattered along the walls. The
mattresses are composed of off-white linings filled with dried grass. At the
footend of each bed stands a small wooden stool with three legs. There are empty
doorframes east, south and west.
name: A Hobwill Room

desc:
The soft earthen walls are about ten feet apart and the ceiling is low. Pale
lichen growns upon the walls, soaking up any condensed water. The room seems to
have no apparent use as it is very empty, a mere hallway almost. Every sound is
carried away long distances into the tunnelsystem.
name: A Hobwill Room

desc:
Heavy is the burden these earthen walls carry. A whole mountain is laid upon
them, and the pressure has formed small cracks in them. Ten small beds stand
along the walls in disorder. Dry grass has been put in old cloth creating soft
mattresses to lie upon.
name: A Hobwill Room

desc:
Here tunnels connect from all directions. The small room is unfurnished save for
a tiny chair in one corner. The air is damp and chill, and from the south comes
the smell of cooked food. There is a tiny draft coming from the eastern exit
drawing out old poluted air.
name: The Tunnel Intersection

desc:
This is a small room, with the main exit west. There is a ventilation shaft in
the ceiling, with a ladder going up to enter it, and a narrow tunnel going
north. A heavy barrel of water is placed in the middle of the room, and water is
trickling down from the ceiling into it.
name: The Ventilation Room

desc:
The air is damp and cold in this dark tunnel. The walls are covered with
condensed water, gathering up into small rivulets running down on the floor.
Small puddles of water can be found inbetween the stones on the ground. The
tunnel bends north towards a larger room, and eastwards to something that
resembles a kitchen.
name: A Bend in the Tunnel

desc:
This is a room set up for preparing food in various forms. There is a basin of
soggy water in the southeast corner and a table filled with different herbs.
Shelves has been carved out in the soggy earthwalls and they are stocked with
cooking utensils. The air is filled with a scent of newly cooked food.
name: The Hobwill Kitchen

desc:
A cramped pathway leads up into the hill northwards. It is dark and the walls
are narrowing in further north. There is a strong draft, the wind is moist and
cold penetrating all and any cloth, skin or fur. The floor is overgrown with
mosses nurturing on the moist and minerals.
name: The Tunnel

desc:
A long tiny shaft has been duged out here, apparently used for the ventilation
of the tunnelsystem beneath. The walls are glistening with water condensing when
it gets in contact with the colder air from the outside. Up ahead there is a
faint light, a promise that the shaft has an ending.
name: The Ventilation Shaft

desc:
The ventilation shaft ends in a cave, facing north. The caveentrance is located
approximately seven yards up a sheer cliff face. A few low bushes would probably
break a fall from this position.
name: The End of the Ventilation Shaft

desc:
The walls of this hole are fairly roughcut, similar to those of the hobwill
tunnels. A ladder, going up and down, has been attached to one of the walls. It
is made out of a red coloured wood, and the damp air has made it quite slippery
and dangerous to climb. There is no movement in the cold air.
name: Under the Barrel

desc:
This is an old, looted crypt. The coffin, where presumably some noble was laid
to rest, is now empty and broken. A rough hole in the ceiling seems to have been
constructed in a differing fashion from the elegant design of the crypt, which
is about ten yards by ten yards in size. There is a doorframe set in the south
wall made in stone with huge hinges, indicating that the now crumbled door was
immense.
name: The Crypt

desc:
A huge doorframe adorns the north wall. A wide corridor runs from east to west,
disappearing into the darkness in both directions. The walls are covered with
elaborate runes of a long forgotten language. In the south wall a small hole
leads off into the darkness beyond. There is an almost palpable sense of evil
all about, and the air is very cold.
name: The Catacombs by the Broken Door

desc:
This is a four-way corridor intersection. All the corridors in this complex are
turning left and right, with stairs going up and down, with three and four-way
intersections everywhere. It is easy to lose any sense of direction. The walls
are supported by large wooden pillars, and the roof is held up by wooden beams
secured to the pillars.
name: The Catacombs

desc:
Massive stone pillars standing in rows along each wall adorns this large hall. A
bold rune has been painted upon the floor in brilliant white, and strangely this
chamber does not share the same air of evil prevalent in the rest of the
underground complex. There is a huge portal north and another exit east, framed
by elaborate sculptures of vicious snakes. The doorway seems to be veiled in
shadows and it is impossible to tell what lies beyond it.
name: The Pillar Room

desc:
This is the large hall of an old Angmar lord. It is fifty by eighty yards, and
lit by dim shining stones set into grasping skeleton hands protruding from the
walls. Towards the northern wall, raised approximately six inches above the
floorlevel, stands an ivory throne. It has no markings. Along the walls there
are broken statues. An exit framed with golden snakes leads south into a system
of corridors, and a large portal leads west.
name: The Wightlord's Chamber

desc:
A small cavern has been hollowed out in the rock. The walls are rough as well as
the floor and roof. Littered about the room are several broken wooden creates
and a few weapon racks and armour pegs. The air is dry and still and not a
single sound can be heard. Against the back wall stands a huge intact stone
chest.
name: The Treasure Chamber

desc:
The moist walls of this corridor are supported by large wooden pillars, upon
which lies wooden beams that holds up the ceiling. The corridor slopes downwards
in a straight north to south direction. A slight wiff in the air brings a foul
scent from the lower parts of the underground system. There is no natural
lightsource here.
name: The Long Slope

desc:
Pillars made of large solid pieces of wood supports the soft earthen walls of
this corridor. Upon each pair of pillar lies a wooden beam that secures the
roof. The corridor continues downwards in a straight north-south direction in
the darkness as no natural light can be found.
name: The Long Slope

desc:
This is a four-way corridor intersection. All the corridors in this complex are
turning left and right, with stairs going up and down, with three and four-way
intersections everywhere. It is easy to lose any sense of direction. The walls
are supported by large wooden pillars, and the roof is held up by wooden beams
secured to the pillars.
name: The Catacombs

desc:
This is a four-way corridor intersection. All the corridors in this complex are
turning left and right, with stairs going up and down, with three and four-way
intersections everywhere. It is easy to lose any sense of direction. The walls
are supported by large wooden pillars, and the roof is held up by wooden beams
secured to the pillars.
name: The Catacombs

desc:
The corridor ends abruptly as it is crossed by a swift stream. The stream is
strong and moving west upstreams is impossible, although it is shallow enough to
allow travel downstream without a boat. There is no dry land on either side of
the stream that has hollowed out the hill on its way east.
name: The Underground Beach

desc:
This is a four-way corridor intersection. All the corridors in this complex are
turning left and right, with stairs going up and down, with three and four-way
intersections everywhere. It is easy to lose any sense of direction. The walls
are supported by large wooden pillars, and the roof is held up by wooden beams
secured to the pillars.
name: The Catacombs

desc:
The corridor continues east to west, with a huge portal set in the south wall.
The portal is painted white and leads to a large hall filled with pillars. A
small draft brings a putrid odor from the south.
name: The Catacombs by the Huge Portal

desc:
This is a four-way corridor intersection. All the corridors in this complex are
turning left and right, with stairs going up and down, with three and four-way
intersections everywhere. It is easy to lose any sense of direction. The walls
are supported by large wooden pillars, and the roof is held up by wooden beams
secured to the pillars.
name: The Catacombs

desc:
This is a four-way corridor intersection. All the corridors in this complex are
turning left and right, with stairs going up and down, with three and four-way
intersections everywhere. It is easy to lose any sense of direction. The walls
are supported by large wooden pillars, and the roof is held up by wooden beams
secured to the pillars.
name: The Catacombs

desc:
The hall, about thirty yards by thirty yards, seems to be a collection point for
sacrifices. Littered all about the stonefloor are skeletons and pieces of ragged
clothes, rusted armours and dented weapons. The eastern exit is adorned with a
large portal while the western exit is framed with golden glowing sculptures of
snakes. North there is a small underground beach.
name: The Sacrificial Hall

desc:
This is a small room, painted all in white, with grey runes on all the walls. A
small portal leads eastwards, and the western exit is framed with golden glowing
sculptures of snakes. A veil of shadows conceils what is hidden behind the
exits.
name: The White Room

desc:
A swift stream of cold water passes by following its course eastwards. There is
no space to stand on either side of the water, except here on the craggy beach.
The water flows quickly and moving west upstreams is impossible, although it is
shallow enough to allow travel downstreams without a boat or raft. A large hall
extends south of here, a part of an old underground system.
name: The Underground Beach

desc:
Deep underneath the hill this tunnel comes to a sudden stop as a solid wall of
stone hinders anyone from digging further ahead. No sunlight ever permeates down
here, creating an everlasting night. The air is very moisty, condensing upon the
cold walls. South of here lies a tunnelcrossing.
name: The End of the Tunnel

desc:
Deep under the mountain four tunnels gather at this point. Small rivulets
gathers the water in small pools in the shallows on the ground. Tiny patches of
moss grows upon the walls, nurturing on the minerals in the water. Any sound
echoes off the walls and amplifies as it travels into the tunnels ahead.
name: The Cave Crossing

desc:
The tunnel gets narrower for a few yards, until it comes to a complete halt.
There is no movement in the air, but it is very damp and crisp. Moss grows in
small patches on the walls and from the ceiling hangs some lichen suspended. Any
noise echoes of the walls and travels ahead in the tunnel.
name: The End of the Tunnel

desc:
This is a three-way intersection point, with tunnels going north, east and
south. The air is chill and moisty, and the moist condenses on the walls,
creating tiny rivulets that gathers in small puddles inbetween the huge stones
and rocks that litters the ground.
name: The Three-way Intersection

desc:
The tunnel is a few feet wide as it abruptly ends with a chasm. The chasm seems
to be bottomless, and its walls are naturally rough. There is no way to descend
down into the chasm as there is no place to secure a climbing rope or hook, and
the walls are slippery and wet as the damp air condenses when it hits the cold
earth.
name: The Chasm

desc:
Four tunnels meet at this point, deep down under the hill where the sun never
permeats, creating an endless night. The walls are rough but soft, and drenched
in water that has condensed from the moist moveless air. Small stones litters
the ground, impeding travel.
name: The Cave Crossing

desc:
The dark tunnel gets smaller and more narrow until it comes to an end. The roof
is low, and the floor is rough. Water condenses upon the cold walls, forming
tiny rivulets that runs down to the ground. It gathers in puddles in the small
hollows in the floor.
name: The End of the Tunnel

desc:
Deep underneath the hill, where the stars never shine, a large hall extends in
all direction. It measures some fifty yards from side to side and the ceiling
reaches about twenty feet up. It is completely and utterly unfurnished, but
there is a distinct smell of something rotten in the chill moveless air.
name: The Cavehall

desc:
The walls and ceiling of this tunnel are roughly cut, and fragments of stone
covers the floor. It continues east and south into the dark heart of the hill.
There is no movement in the chill air, but it still makes its way through cloth,
fur and skin. Lichen grows in abundance on the soft walls, nurturing on minerals
and the damp air.
name: The Cavetunnel

desc:
The pitch-black tunnel comes to a sudden stop without any explanatory reason.
Water trickles down the cold slippery walls in intricate patterns, hits the
floor and continues along the sides westwards. The passageway is narrow and the
air stale and moist. To the west, the tunnel enters an underground crossing.
name: The End of the Tunnel

desc:
The tunnelwalls are rough and composed of soft earth. There is a hole in the
eastern wall. It is without any specific shape and has ragged edges, as if
either natural, or hewn by one who had no care for its form. The cavetunnel
turns north and west.
name: The Cavetunnel

desc:
This small room seems to be a natural cavity, with a very small crack in the
western wall. The floor is litered with dirt and old dried out snakeskins,
aswell as faeces in different sizes, all originating from snakes. There is no
natural lightsource in this part of the cavesystem.
name: The Snakepit

desc:
Fragments of stone covers the floor of the cave. The walls and the ceiling are
roughly cut, tunneling into the dark heart of the hill. The air is still, damp
and cold, making its way through any cloth, fur or skin. The cavetunnel splits
here, and continues north, south and west.
name: The Cavetunnel

desc:
The walls are very rough and composed of earth. The cave is fairly large, with a
distance from the floor to the ceiling of about six feet. There is something
written on the wall, and a large white rune has been painted on the floor. The
cave continues south, and northwards lies the catacombs.
name: The Entrance to the Cave

desc:
The walls are just a few feet apart only allowing movement in a single file. The
air is damp and condenses upon the cold earthen walls, forming tiny riulets that
flows down on the floor. There they gather in the shallows creating small
puddles. The passageway gets more narrow further north, where it ends with a
metal hatch.
name: The Hatch in the Tunnel

desc:
The stonewell continues up and down from this point. The top is reachable, and
about ten feet down there is a ledge that might be able to support a person.
There is a slight draft coming from the bottom of the well, with a scent of
decay, and any sound echoes back much stronger.
name: In the Well

desc:
The dark tunnel twists and turns as it makes its way through the hill. There is
a strong draft in the damp and crisp air. Small patches of moss grows on the
walls, and lichen is suspended from the ceiling. Any noise echoes of the walls
and travels ahead in the tunnel.
name: The Tunnel

desc:
This room is twenty by twenty yards with the ceiling about five yards up. The
walls, initially painted with light and colourful nature scenes, have now been
painted with vertical stripes of black concealing most of the initial painting.
There are identical exits north, south, east and west. The exits are framed with
glowing golden sculptures of snakes. A veil of shadows conceals what is hidden
behind the exits.
name: The Noble Room

desc:
The room is twenty by twenty yards with the ceiling about five yards up. The
walls, initially painted with light and colourful scenery of the nature, have
now been painted with vertical stripes of black concealing most of the initial
painting. There are identical exits north, south, east and west. The exits are
framed with golden glowing sculptures of snakes. A veil of shadows conceals what
is hidden behind the exits.
name: The Noble Room

desc:
The room is twenty by twenty yards with the ceiling about five yards up. The
walls, initially painted with light and colourful scenery of the nature, has now
been painted with vertical stripes of black conceiling most of the initial
painting. There are identical exits north, south, east and west. The exits are
framed with golden glowing sculptures of snakes. A veil of shadows conceils what
is hidden behind the exits.
name: The Noble Room

desc:
The room is twenty by twenty yards with the ceiling about five yards up. The
walls, initially painted with light and colourful scenery of the nature, has now
been painted with vertical stripes of black conceiling most of the initial
painting. There are identical exits north, south, east and west. The exits are
framed with golden glowing sculptures of snakes. A veil of shadows conceils what
is hidden behind the exits.
name: The Noble Room

desc:
The room is twenty by twenty yards with the ceiling about five yards up. The
walls, initially painted with light and colourful scenery of the nature, has now
been painted with vertical stripes of black conceiling most of the initial
painting. There are identical exits north, south, east and west. The exits are
framed with golden glowing sculptures of snakes. A veil of shadows conceils what
is hidden behind the exits.
name: The Noble Room

desc:
The room is twenty by twenty yards with the ceiling about five yards up. The
walls, initially painted with light and colourful scenery of the nature, has now
been painted with vertical stripes of black conceiling most of the initial
painting. There are identical exits north, south, east and west. The exits are
framed with golden glowing sculptures of snakes. A veil of shadows conceils what
is hidden behind the exits.
name: The Noble Room

desc:
The beginnings of a road or well-travelled path start here and head to the
south. Smooth tree trunks stuck into the dirt side-by-side form a wall on both
sides of the path that expand around shadowy buildings farther in the abandoned
town. Several parts of the wall are missing and the pieces that remain are
marred by strike marks.
name: Entrance to the Ruins

desc:
This is a large courtyard between an old stable and a destroyed watchtower. A
stone well stands in the middle of the area. Some of the rectangular stones are
missing from the rim of the well. The water has a layer of dust covering the
surface. A road continues to the south through the town.
name: Murky Well

desc:
Partially broken water troughs and piles of collapsed wood on rotting hay
indicate this was once where travellers tied their horses while they attended to
business in the town. It is a convenient distance to the well to the west for
keeping the water troughs full of water.
name: Ruined Stables

desc:
Long, round wooden poles lie in the dirt with a small, square roof collapsed in
a heap. This appears to be the remains of a watchtower for the small town. Burn
marks on the ends of some pieces of wood indicate it may have suffered
structural damage from a fire.
name: Collapsed Watchtower

desc:
This room is about six feet tall, and is recessed into the earth. The upper half
of this structure is built of logs and mortar. The walls of the lower half are
made of stones pressed into the earth. The floor is flat, dry, compact dirt. A
stone slab rises a foot high in the centre of the room. Scattered about are
bones and shards of clay pottery. A portion of the southern wall has collapsed,
exposing a passage.
name: Earthen Barrow

desc:
A short tunnel from the west opens to a narrow chamber that was dug within the
surrounding hill ages ago. Flat, grey stones hold the earth at bay. Water has
seeped in creating trails of mud running down the walls in several places. A
flattened mound of smooth rocks, roughly six feet long and three feet wide,
marks what was meant to be the final resting place of an ancient noble. It now
sits empty.
name: Narrow Chamber within a Hill

desc:
This ancient temple was built into the hillside eons ago. The floor, walls and
ceiling are all crafted of rough-cut stone bricks. The structure looks strong in
spite of its age. Chains hang from wooden rafters across the tall ceiling, from
which hang two lit lanterns. A thick layer of dust covers the floor, though it
has been disturbed as footprints can be seen leading to and from deeper into the
temple to the south.
name: Entrance of an Ancient Temple

desc:
This long, dusty hall built of rough-cut stone runs north and south. The stone
walls are bare save for empty torch sconces covered by spider webs. Every sound
echoes throughout the passage. The air is stale and musty, though it smells more
fresh towards the north.
name: Dusty Passage

desc:
Six stone pillars rise up to meet heavy wooden rafters supporting a twenty foot
high ceiling. Bare walls allow every sound to echo throughout the spacious room.
The remains of broken wooden chairs are scattered across the floor. The end of
one table has been cleared of dust and debris, and looks to have been recently
used. A short staircase leads up to a dais south of here.
name: Ruined Common Room

desc:
This room was once dedicated to the care of the deceased. A stone table and a
copper tub sit near the centre of the chamber, a tattered ornate rug on the
floor between them. Shelves on the walls still hold a few clay jars, but many of
these vessels have been broken. Shards of pottery are scattered about.
name: Ancient Mortuary

desc:
A dais of three steps rises a few feet above a large common room to the north.
Its steps are adorned with a relief sculpture displaying the sun at different
times of the day rising and lowering over a field of workers. Tattered
tapestries hang on the walls, a thick layer of mould and dust hiding any colour
or imagery they once contained.
name: Cracked Stone Altar

desc:
The air here is stale, dank, and unmoving. The walls of this passage are rough
cut from the surrounding stone. The floor is littered with bones and debris.
Thick dust hangs in the air, making it difficult to see very far. Some sort of
chute can be made out in the ceiling leading upward, but there is no way to
access it.
name: Fusty Passage

desc:
The air is thick with dust that floats almost unmoving and obscures vision.
Several skeletons, mostly intact and clothed, litter the floor. This passage was
cut roughly from the surrounding stone, possibly as a source of bricks for other
structures. There are dark recesses in the walls that light does not easily
penetrate, but all appear to be empty.
name: End of a Fusty Passage

desc:
The western wall of this passage shows it was roughly cut from the surrounding
stone. In stark contrast, an ornate archway lined with grey brick opens to a
room to the east. The air is cold and unmoving, filled with thick dust that
makes it hard to see. The main passage continues north and south.
name: Dank, Branching Passage

desc:
The floor of the passage is littered with stone debris, its source being a
gaping hole in the brick wall to the east. The smell of fresh air comes from
that direction, in contrast to the musty smell and thick clouds of dust that
seem to fill the passages north and west of here.
name: Intersection in an Underground Passage

desc:
The air is thick with dust that floats almost unmoving and obscures vision. This
passage was cut roughly from the surrounding stone, possibly as a source of
bricks for other structures. Set into the north wall is a simple wooden door.
name: End of a Passage

desc:
This small chamber has only one entrance, a simple wooden door in the south
wall. The walls and ceiling of this room are made of stone that has been
blackened by a layer of soot, and footprints of dried mud criss cross the floor.
Otherwise, the room is tidy, with sparse furnishings kept in order and showing
signs of recent use.
name: Blackened Chamber

desc:
This tomb is recessed six feet into the ground. A short stairway in the eastern
wall leads to a heavy, immovable stone slab. The floor of the tomb is littered
by stone debris, as both the ceiling and the western wall have collapsed,
leaving gaping holes. Two empty stone coffins adorn the walls, their lids
leaning against them.
name: Cluttered Tomb

desc:
Arches made of black brick curve ornately up the walls to the ceiling. Built
into the walls between the arches are twelve stone sarcophagi, all of them
looking untouched and sealed. Dust hangs densely in the air here. Arched
doorways lead to the east and west.
name: Underground Crypt

desc:
A deep hole in the ground rises above and below. Its walls are damp, hard packed
earth. Large, shelf-life fungi grow from the walls, providing unusual foot and
hand holds for anyone wishing to traverse this passage. The air is still and
musty, smelling more dank below, but fresher above.
name: Damp Sinkhole

desc:
The bottom of this deep sinkhole is littered with rotting leaves and branches,
making the floor soft and uneven. Large, shelf-like fungi grow from the walls,
while thin, white fungi with rounded caps grow from the floor like tall grasses.
The air is damp and still, with an overbearing musty odour.
name: Bottom of a Sinkhole

desc:
Unlike the barrow to the north, this earthy hollow was not made by man. Roots
have forced their way through the smooth dirt ceiling and walls in several
places, providing many places for spiders to build their webs and small animals
to burrow and hide.
name: Dry Earthy Hollow

desc:
Wheel ruts are vaguely identifiable in the dirt but are obscured by caked mud
and wooden debris scattered everywhere. The broken and splintered wood pieces
appear to be from the nearby buildings to the west and east as they are only
partially standing. Sharp shadows are created by the ruins of the buildings.
name: Street filled with Debris

desc:
The deeper into the old town the more debris and broken building materials are
piled in the dirt. Ashes that cooled long ago have drifted into cracks and mix
with the dirt creating a kind of grey mud paste. The road through town curves to
the west surrounded by shadowy ruins.
name: Road through Andrath

desc:
The road running through town ends here with more open space in front of the
ruins of several buildings. A wagon with all the wheels broken lies in the dirt
next to a ruined building to the south that appears to have been a large home.
Other buildings at the end of the road have been turned to piles of rotting wood
and squared stones.
name: End of the Road

desc:
Collapsed tables and frayed canvas stretch out on the ground as evidence of a
former market. Most of the stalls appeared open to the air. Some broken baskets
contain a dark, putrid sludge that was must have once been fresh vegetables or
fruit left to rot.
name: Market Square

desc:
The long, wooden bar, where ale was once poured and stories were told, is now a
silent reminder of a bygone era. Shattered glass from cups and bottles collects
dust on the floor and a the bar. Stains from dried liquids run down the wood and
leave vague circles on the floor. Some broken stools and tables are scattered
around the edges of the room but it appears most of the furniture was removed.
name: Abandoned Tavern

desc:
Walls that once separated the area into rooms for privacy have almost completely
been destroyed. The remains of several simple beds lie broken in the debris.
Torn linens grow mould in the corners. The exterior walls are intact enough to
prevent things from entering except from the old tavern to the west. A section
of roof attached to the tavern still covers part of the interior. The ceiling
nearest to the east edge of the building is completely missing.
name: Remains of an Inn

desc:
This was once a two-story house but the entire second floor has been destroyed.
Enough wood of the second story floor remains to cover most of the first floor
as a roof. The only remnants in the building are pieces of broken furniture and
other debris that is difficult to identify. A small hatch in the floor is
visible in one corner.
name: Old House

desc:
Against the eastern wall, a row of large barrels stand in various states of
disrepair. Despite being empty, they still smell strongly of wine. A few crushed
wooden boxes are scattered on the otherwise empty floor. Like other buildings in
the village, the roof has long been absent and allowed the elements to begin
wearing away the interior.
name: Roofless Storeroom

desc:
Perhaps this house once belonged to the most powerful person of Andrath at one
point in time. Tall columns partially stand in front of the house and some
detailed carvings are still visible on wooden beams lying on the floor. The
walls of the home have lost significant integrity and lean to the west as if the
next strong wind could blow everything down.
name: Demolished House

desc:
The ceiling in this tunnel consists of the floorboards and support beams for the
building above. Tiny flecks of dust waft slowly down from between the boards on
to the dirt floor. The walls are rough with marks from picks and shovels. The
floor is exceptionally flat.
name: Dirty Tunnel

desc:
The tunnel generally curves between the north and south but meanders back and
forth between those points. Most of the tunnel is large enough for one or two
persons side-by-side with one area that is large enough to accommodate a group
of people. Muddy puddles formed in the large room where the depth of the tunnel
apparently bumps against the water table.
name: Curving Tunnel

desc:
Baskets and small barrels with rusting bands are stacked around the room. An
expansive layer of mould grows in soft tufts on the remains of vegetables and
potatoes. The floor is dry dirt and mostly level as it seems the most care was
taken there as opposed to the roughly-carved walls. Narrow tunnels to the north,
east and south are obscured in darkness.
name: Crude Root Cellar

desc:
A short tunnel leads to an unusually large room with impenetrable darkness. The
gloom intrudes the senses with a heavy weight. It is hard to find the walls of
the area as they are indistinguishable from the blackness of the air. A thick
smell of damp decay makes breathing difficult.
name: Choking Darkness

desc:
The roof of the tunnel is low enough to make stooping necessary for most
individuals traversing the area. Some areas are narrow while others expand into
larger round rooms. It seems most of the tunnel was formed naturally as the
walls are mostly smooth rock and dirt. The tunnel ends abruptly with only some
small unusual iron tools partially buried at the bottom of the final wall.
name: Low Tunnel

desc:
An odd tunnel has been formed here underground, and it continues off to the
west. The walls and ceiling are lined with roots, and the ground is bare and
dusty. Peculiar noises emanate from the west, echoing quietly throughout the
tunnel. The roots lining the ceiling above appear to block passage in that
direction.
name: Root-lined Tunnel

desc:
A narrow, cramped tunnel has been formed underground here, spanning off to the
east. The walls and ceiling are covered in roots, but the floor is bare dirt.
The roots above can be moved easily to expose an accessway to a brush-filled
valley above.
name: Western End of a Root-lined Tunnel

desc:
Steep, rocky walls rise up in the west, south, and east, covered in hanging
vines and roots. The terrain opens to the north, where the vegetation becomes
thicker. A carpet of thick roots on the ground blocks a passageway below. A
strong gust of wind blows into the valley from the north.
name: Brush-filled Valley

desc:
The valley walls bend southward and westward. Dense, thorny brush grows high
overhead with thorny vines hanging down. Vines and roots creep down the rocky
walls of the valley. The ground is dry and cracked, and strong wind gusts blow
in from the west.
name: Brush-filled Valley

desc:
The brush-filled valley branches and splits off to the north, west, and east.
Dense, thorny brush grows abundantly all about. The valley walls are rocky and
steep, with roots protruding through the rocks here and there. The ground is
bare dirt and covered in roots. A blustery wind blows in from the west.
name: Brush-filled Valley

desc:
The valley branches off to the west, east, and south. The vegetation here is dry
and dense, filled with thorns and spines. The earthen valley walls are dry and
covered in dirt and crumbling rock, with the occasional roots sticking out. A
strong gust of wind blows in from west to east.
name: Brush-filled Valley

desc:
The valley dead-ends here, but opens to the east. Patches of grass form here,
breaking up the otherwise dull landscape. Dry vegetation is seen all about, and
clumps of dirt tumble down the valley walls to the ground below. Thick roots
cover the ground, even where grass is growing.
name: Brush-filled Valley

desc:
The valley continues to the north, south, and west, with a tall, rocky, earthen
wall to the east. Blue thistle flowers grow all throughout the dense vegetation
here, which is covered in sharp thorns and spines that hang down. The ground
here is cracked, dry and bare.
name: Brush-filled Valley

desc:
The brush here is thick and dense, and travel is daunting and perilous. Adding
to the difficulty of travel, roots cover the ground, waiting to ensnare unaware
travellers. Creeping thistle weeds, with bright blue heads, grow all around in
sporadic patches.
name: Deep Brush

desc:
The valley dead-ends to the south, but opens to the north. Dense, dry vegetation
fills the landscape all around. The ground underfoot is bare dirt. Clumps of
dirt and rock tumble down the valley walls, and blue thistle flowers grow all
over the valley floor.
name: Brush-filled Dead-end

desc:
The valley dead-ends to the west, north, and south, but opens up to the east.
Dense, dry vegetation, covered in thorns, fills the landscape and hinders
travel. The ground is dry and cracked, and the walls of the valley are earthen
and covered in rocks, which occasionally tumble down to the valley floor.
name: Brush-filled Dead-end

desc:
The brushy valley comes to a dead-end in every direction except to the west,
where it opens up into a dense, brushy landscape. The vegetation here is thin
and grass grows on the ground in patches. Vines and roots hang down from the
earthen valley walls, and a tree grows horizontally, high above from the wall to
the east.
name: Brushy Dead-end

desc:
The thickness of the brush obscures the view in most directions. The earthen
wall to the north, west, and south only becomes noticeable when in immediate
proximity. The valley opens to the east, where the brush, while still thick,
thins slightly. All about, blue thistle flowers grow in abundance.
name: Thick Brush
